topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical blows, searing my retinas. I stood before the canvas, a violent splash of crimson and charcoal that was undeniably mine, now framed, hung on a wall in a room full of polite, vacant-eyed patrons. A tremor ran through my hands, and I clenched my jaw so tightly my molars ached. "This is mine," I hissed to the silence, my voice thin and brittle. The curator smiled, a practiced, saccharine gesture that grated on my nerves like sandpaper. I took a step forward, the polished floor reflecting my agitated silhouette. I wanted to rip the frame from the wall, to shatter the glass and let the raw energy of the painting spill out onto the floor, a chaotic protest against this invasion.

[story 2]

I watched the crowd mill about, their murmurs a low, irritating hum that vibrated unpleasantly in my chest. Every glance seemed to be an assessment, a silent judgment of my very existence. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of my worn sketchbook. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks a deep, furious red. "How dare they," I muttered under my breath, my tone sharp enough to cut glass. I wanted to scream, to unleash the churning resentment building behind my ribs. I took a deep, shuddering breath, trying to anchor myself, but the air felt too thick, too suffocating. I started pacing, a tight, frantic circuit around the display, my eyes darting from the painting to the indifferent faces staring back.

[story 3]

The silence in the room was deafening, a heavy blanket smothering any semblance of peace. I felt a distinct, internal tightening, a coiled tension ready to snap. When the gallery owner began a tedious explanation of the piece's provenance, I found myself tapping my foot relentlessly against the marble floor, a rapid, uneven rhythm. My gaze fixed on the painting—my vision of it, distorted by the glossy varnish and the judgmental spotlights. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped me, followed by a low, guttural sound that was half-choke, half-snarl. I turned abruptly, my shoulders rigid, and began to dismantle the space with my body language, a slow, deliberate withdrawal, every muscle vibrating with suppressed intensity.
